point_001: The title and abstract set overly ambitious expectations ('human-level prompt engineering') that the actual results do not fully support, particularly given that the method underperforms human-written prompts on harder tasks like Membership and Second Letter.
point_002: The evaluation tasks are too simple and surface-level (e.g., single-word translation, syntax-level NLP tasks, GLUE tasks already solved at 90%+ accuracy), making it unclear whether the approach generalizes to more complex reasoning or deeper language understanding tasks.
point_003: The paper relies almost exclusively on the benchmark from Honovich et al. (2022) without sufficient justification for why this is the appropriate benchmark, and without testing on more established or challenging benchmarks such as GLUE, BIG-Bench, summarization, intent classification, or slot filling.
point_004: The core idea of using LLMs to automatically generate and select prompts from input/output pairs was already introduced by Honovich et al. (2022); the novelty of this work is primarily the search/filtering/selection pipeline on top of that prior framework.
point_005: The assumptions and intuitions in Section 3.1 are insufficiently explained—for example, why the model should predict missing context before demonstrations, and what 'more versatile approach' means in practice.
point_006: It is unclear how multiple candidate instructions generated per input/output pair are consolidated: which instruction is ultimately chosen at inference time, and whether all top candidates are used together in an in-context learning scenario.
point_007: The paper lacks concrete worked examples (e.g., actual input/output pairs, generated instructions, zero-shot vs. few-shot scenarios) in the figures and task descriptions, which would substantially aid readability.
point_008: No code or implementation details are provided to ensure reproducibility of the experiments.
point_009: The paper does not explore genuinely difficult prompt engineering challenges—such as cases where humans struggle to find effective prompts—which would better validate the claim of human-level performance.
